6: heis theos ho pater ex hou ta panta kai hemeis eis auton kai heis kyrios Iesous Christos di hou ta panta kai hemeis di autou: Paul's two-clause Christological declaration is a deliberate restructuring of the Shema (Deuteronomy 6:4: YHWH our God, YHWH is one). Paul does not replace the Shema but expands it: the 'one God' (Father) and the 'one Lord' (Jesus Christ) together constitute the Shema's unity. The prepositions mark distinct roles: ex hou (from whom) for the Father as source; di hou (through whom) for the Son as agent/mediator. This is one of Paul's most explicit pre-Nicene Trinitarian formulas, set within a polemical argument about idols — the God who is one now includes Jesus Christ.
1: peri de tōn eidōlothytōn (now about food offered to idols) — the fifth Corinthian topic introduced by peri de. Eidōlothyton (idol-meat): food sacrificed to a deity in a temple context, then available for purchase in the market or served at civic/social banquets. The issue was pervasive in Greco-Roman urban life; most social occasions involved food in some way connected to temple sacrifice. gnōsis physioī, hē de agapē oikodomeī (knowledge puffs up, but love builds up) — physioō (puff up, inflate): Paul reuses the Corinthian-disease vocabulary from 1 Corinthians 4:6; 1 Corinthians 4:18; 1 Corinthians 5:2. Agapē oikodomeī (love builds up): oikodomeō is Paul's primary communal-building metaphor (1 Corinthians 3:9-15; 1 Corinthians 14:3-5). Translation note flags agapē as distinct from philia and erōs.
2: ei tis dokei egnōkenai ti, oupō egnō kathōs dei gnōnai (anyone who thinks they know something does not yet know as they ought to know) — the two gnō forms contrast: egnōkenai (to have known: the arrogant self-assessment) and egnō kathōs dei gnōnai (to know as one ought to know: the standard not yet reached). Paul does not deny that knowledge is possible but reframes what it means to truly know: genuine knowledge is always accompanied by the awareness of one's remaining ignorance.
3: ei de tis agapā ton Theon, houtos egnōstai hyp' autou (but if anyone loves God, this person is known by God) — the shift from knowing to being known: the passive egnōstai (has been known) makes the relationship asymmetric in an important direction. It is not that the one who loves God thereby knows God better (though they may); it is that they are known by God — which is the ground of the relationship. This echoes Paul's argument in Galatians 4:9: you have come to know God, or rather to be known by God.
4: ouden eidōlon en kosmō (an idol has no real existence in the world) — ouden (nothing, no reality): the idol is nothing because there is no corresponding divine reality behind it. oudeis Theos ei mē heis (there is no God but one) — the Shema-form of Jewish monotheism (Deuteronomy 6:4) adapted: Jewish piety expressed precisely this conviction about other gods' non-existence. The Corinthian "strong" who have this gnōsis are theologically correct.
5: legoumenoi theoi (so-called gods) — Paul's ironic quotation marks: he does not deny the widespread social reality of polytheism (there are indeed many entities worshipped as gods and lords) but refuses them the unqualified title. Eite en ouranō eite epi gēs (whether in heaven or on earth) — the cosmic register of the claim: even the highest celestial powers are merely legomenoi (called, named) gods, not actually divine.
7: synētheia tou eidōlou heōs arti (through their long association with idols, literally their habit of the idol until now) — synētheia (custom, long-established association, habit): this is not intellectual confusion but deeply embedded behavioral memory. The person's conscience (syneidēsis) has been formed by years of treating idol-meat as genuinely sacred. Even after conversion and intellectual assent to monotheism, the emotional-behavioral reflex remains. asthenēs ousa molynestai (being weak, is defiled) — the weak conscience is defiled not because the food is objectively defiling but because the person eats it with the consciousness of it as truly offered to a god.
8: brōma de hēmas ou parastisei tō Theō (food does not bring us closer to God) — the neutral status of food: neither eating nor abstaining makes one closer to or further from God. This is the "strong" position that Paul actually affirms — the knowledge of the strong is correct. The argument that follows (1 Corinthians 8:9-13) does not refute the gnōsis but asks whether the gnōsis is being exercised in a way that builds or destroys.
9: blepete de mē pōs hē exousia hymōn autē proskomma genētai tois asthenousi (but be careful that this right of yours does not become a stumbling block to the weak) — exousia (right, authority, freedom): the word Paul uses throughout 1 Cor 7-10 for legitimate freedom. Proskomma (stumbling block, obstacle): the same word as Romans 14:13 — the exercise of legitimate freedom becomes a moral obstacle for the weaker believer.
10: oidomelēthēsetai eis to ta eidōlothyta esthiein (won't they be encouraged/built up to eat idol-meat?) — the irony of the verb: oikodomeō (build up) is Paul's positive term for communal edification (1 Corinthians 8:1; 1 Corinthians 14:3), but here it denotes a destructive building — the weak believer is being built up in the direction of their destruction. Seeing the strong Christian eating in the temple precinct encourages them to participate in a way that violates their own conscience.
11: apollytai gar ho asthenōn en tē sē gnōsei, ho adelphos di' hon Christos apethanen (so this weak brother, for whom Christ died, is destroyed by your knowledge) — the Christological weight of the argument: the weak believer is not merely a social inconvenience but one for whom Christ died (di' hon Christos apethanen). The death of Christ for the weak establishes the infinite value of the weak believer's conscience-integrity. Destroying that believer with food is destroying someone whom Christ valued with his life.
12: hamartanete eis ton Christon (you are sinning against Christ) — the escalation: sinning against the weak brother's conscience is sinning against Christ himself. Typtontes autōn tēn syneidēsin asthenousan (wounding their weak conscience) — typtō (strike, wound): the violence of the metaphor captures the real damage done to the weak believer's moral integrity when they are pressured by example to violate their own conscience.
13: dioper ei brōma skandalizei ton adelphon mou, ou mē phagō krea eis ton aiōna (therefore, if what I eat causes my brother to stumble, I will never eat meat again) — skandalizō (cause to stumble, trip): the foundation of Paul's practical conclusion. The indefinite extension (eis ton aiōna, forever) is rhetorical hyperbole but makes the principle absolute: Paul's freedom over food is entirely subordinate to the welfare of the weak believer. The argument of 1 Corinthians 8 is completed in 1 Corinthians 9 (Paul's own example of waiving rights) and 1 Corinthians 10 (practical guidance on idol-meat situations).
